stories.title: AI Used to Pinpoint Whale Heat Signatures to Prevent Ship Collisions in San Francisco Bay
stories.summary: Heat-sensing AI called Whale Spotter is now detecting grey whales in San Francisco Bay and alerting ships to avoid collisions. Within an hour of installation, it spotted 180 whale blows, offering a cutting-edge solution to protect migrating whales from deadly ship strikes.
stories.extended_summary: An hour after plugging in the first Whale Spotter device on Angel Island, researchers saw 180 whale blows—the warm breath of grey whales captured by thermal imaging against the cold ocean. The AI instantly uploads these heat signatures to a digital map that mariners can access in real time, helping them navigate safely around the whales. With ship strikes being a leading cause of whale mortality, this innovative system could be a game-changer for conservation in busy urban bays.
stories.headline_split: AI Used to Pinpoint Whale Heat Signatures to Prevent
stories.fake_headline: Community Garden in Detroit Provides Free Organic Vegetables to 500 Families Weekly

stories.title: Ride It Out: How Cycling Helps Kids Focus
stories.summary: A cycling class at a Wisconsin middle school is helping students with ADHD improve focus and behavior. Teacher observations and research support that even a short ride can transform a distracted student into a calm, engaged learner.
stories.extended_summary: Jimmy G., a fifth grader with ADHD, used to be disruptive and unfocused until he started a cycling class through the Outride Riding for Focus program. His teacher noticed a dramatic change: after riding, he sits down, stays on task, and gets right to work. The program, founded by Specialized bike founder Mike Sinyard (who also has ADHD), is backed by cognitive science showing that exercise boosts attention. For Jimmy, cycling has become a game-changer that even complements his medication.
fake_endings: Why Kids Learn Better on Bikes
stories.fake_headline: Chef Turns Food Waste into Gourmet Meals for Shelters, Saves 10,000 Pounds Monthly

stories.title: The case for making prison phone calls free: new data, real results
stories.summary: Making prison phone calls free has saved families over $622 million and dramatically increased contact between incarcerated people and loved ones. New data shows the policy reduces harm and benefits Black and brown communities most.
stories.extended_summary: When states and counties switched from revenue-sharing deals with telecom providers to direct contracts, the cost of prison phone calls dropped by over 60%. A report from Worth Rises found that daily call usage in prisons jumped from 25 to 45 minutes, and in jails it more than doubled. Families—disproportionately Black and brown—saved more than $622 million, and 6.4 billion additional minutes of connection were logged. Regular contact with family is proven to reduce recidivism and improve outcomes, making free calls a transformative policy.
stories.headline_split: The case for making prison phone calls free:
stories.fake_headline: City Opens Free Legal Clinic for Immigrants, Processes 1,000 Cases in First Year

stories.title: Louvre Announces Mona Lisa Will Have Her Own Building in Major Redesign to Ease Tourist Scrum
stories.summary: The Louvre is moving the Mona Lisa to a separate 33,000 sq ft wing to ease overcrowding. The $1 billion renovation will let selfie-takers get in and out without disturbing other visitors, and modernize the museum's infrastructure. A win for everyone.
stories.extended_summary: For the first time, the world's most famous painting will have its own home. By moving the Mona Lisa to a dedicated 33,000-square-foot wing, the Louvre plans to end the daily crush of 20,000 admirers jostling for a glimpse. Those who only want to see the painting can bypass the rest of the museum, while art lovers exploring the collections won't have to wait in line behind Mona Lisa-only crowds. The $1 billion renovation, announced in a press conference, aims to modernize the entire museum and dramatically reduce congestion.
stories.headline_split: Louvre Announces Mona Lisa Will Have Her Own Building
fake_endings: to Make Room for a New Modern Art Wing
stories.fake_headline: 92-Year-Old Veteran Completes Bucket List After Skydiving and Swimming With Dolphins

stories.title: Philanthropy’s Most Important Metric Is the One It Never Measures (Blog)
stories.summary: In 2025, the Abdulla Al Ghurair Hub for Digital Teaching and Learning at AUB quietly reached its break-even point and was replicated by the university without further foundation funding. This outcome – lasting structural change – is rarely measured in philanthropy but may be its most important metric.
stories.extended_summary: A university in Beirut quietly hit a milestone no one was tracking: the cumulative break-even point on a philanthropically funded initiative. Even amid currency collapse, the aftermath of the port explosion, and regional conflict, the American University of Beirut independently decided to replicate the Abdulla Al Ghurair Hub for Digital Teaching and Learning across additional departments. The hub, an institutional nerve center for online education, outgrew its need for foundation support. This story highlights a category of philanthropic success that remains unmeasured: building something permanent that outlasts the grant itself.
stories.fake_headline: Grandmother Knits 500 Hats for Premature Babies in Year-Long Charity Drive

stories.title: This Philly Barbecue Joint Is Built on Second Chances
stories.summary: Southern Flames BBQ in Philadelphia hires formerly incarcerated individuals, at-risk youth, and disabled veterans, giving them second chances. Owner Rev. James Buck has built a thriving business while providing stable jobs to those often overlooked by society.
stories.extended_summary: When Rev. James Buck visits a state prison to recruit, he leaves with five pages of names—people ready for release and needing work. Since 2020, his Southern Flames BBQ has employed over 40 formerly incarcerated individuals, along with at-risk youth and disabled veterans. Despite the perceived risk, the restaurant has always been profitable, proving that hiring people labeled as unemployable can be both socially impactful and good for business. One cook, Manny West, says finding that job changed his life.
stories.headline_split: This Philly Barbecue Joint Is Built on
stories.fake_headline: Teacher Starts School Garden That Feeds 200 Families, Wins National Award

stories.title: Amsterdam strips meat and fossil fuel ads from its public spaces
stories.summary: Amsterdam became the first city in the world to ban public advertising for meat and fossil fuel products, effective May 1. Billboards and tram shelters now feature cultural events instead. The policy aims to halve meat consumption and achieve carbon neutrality by 2050.
stories.extended_summary: Since May 1, Amsterdam's billboards and tram shelters have swapped chicken nuggets and SUVs for the Rijksmuseum and piano concerts. The Dutch capital is the first city worldwide to ban public advertising for both meat and fossil fuel products. Councillors from the GreenLeft Party and Party for the Animals co-authored the restrictions, framing dietary choice as a climate issue. "We're giving people more freedom by stopping big companies from telling us what to eat," says Anke Bakker, the politician who drove the ban. The policy is part of Amsterdam's goal to halve meat consumption and reach carbon neutrality by 2050.
stories.headline_split: Amsterdam strips meat and fossil fuel ads
fake_endings: from its billboards and bus stops
in a move to promote sustainability
stories.fake_headline: Vienna Installs Solar Panels on All Historic Buildings Without Affecting Aesthetic

stories.title: Growing Coffee in the World’s Densest City: Hong Kong Roastery Hails Beans That Aren’t Imported
stories.summary: Hong Kong's Lantau Island is now home to a working coffee farm, proving that premium Arabica can grow in the world's densest city. The Lantau Coffee Co-Op turns a passion project into locally grown pride.
stories.extended_summary: Ringo Lam, a former tech entrepreneur, planted 100 coffee seeds from Panama on Lantau Island—and 80 sprouted. Despite Hong Kong's lack of altitude and space, the Lantau Coffee Co-Op now produces Arabica beans at just under 1,000 meters elevation. Katie Chick, a university researcher, helps run a farm on Hong Kong island itself, yielding about 50 kilograms of beans from 800 trees. The operation challenges assumptions about urban agriculture and proves that with determination, coffee can thrive even in the most unlikely place.
stories.headline_split: Growing Coffee in the World’s Densest City:
fake_endings: Hong Kong's Urban Farm Produces First Batch
Local Rooftop Coffee Beans Hit Market
stories.fake_headline: Teacher Turns School Roof into Vegetable Garden, Feeds 300 Students Daily

stories.title: Superhot Rock Holds the Energy of the Future
stories.summary: A startup called Mazama Energy is developing superhot rock geothermal power at Oregon's Newberry Volcano. By injecting water into rock temperatures of 300-400°C, they create supercritical water that can generate massive amounts of clean energy. Recent tech advances are making geothermal more affordable and accessible.
stories.extended_summary: Two miles beneath Oregon's Newberry Volcano, a new energy revolution is heating up. Mazama Energy is pioneering superhot rock geothermal, injecting water into rock at 300-400°C to create supercritical water – a state with the properties of both liquid and gas – that can drive turbines at unprecedented efficiency. "Superhot rock is everywhere, but in Newberry it is very shallow," says director of communications Pete Lumley. While geothermal has existed for decades, recent technology advances are skyrocketing its output while cutting costs. This could be a game-changer for the nation's green energy transition.
fake_endings: the Key to a Carbon-Free World
stories.fake_headline: Retired Engineer Builds Solar-Powered Boat to Clean Plastic From Rivers

stories.title: The English restaurant turning hospitality on its head
stories.summary: In Stroud, England, The Long Table restaurant operates on a pay-what-you-can model with no questions asked. Guests pay what they can afford, sit family-style, and enjoy a warm community atmosphere. The restaurant brings together locals from all walks of life for good food and radical hospitality.
stories.extended_summary: On a cool evening in the Cotswolds, an old industrial building buzzes with life. Children weave between benches, a local band tunes up, and cutlery clinks as strangers share long wooden tables. This is The Long Table, a restaurant where radical hospitality meets good food. The menu offers just one or two dishes, seating is communal, and payment is entirely voluntary: pay what you can afford, no questions asked. If you can pay more than the suggested price, you're invited to; if you need to pay less or nothing, you're equally welcome. It's a model that's bringing together families, students, retirees, and after-work crowds in a spirit of generosity.
stories.fake_headline: Family-Run Eatery in Naples Feeds 1000 Homeless Every Christmas for 50 Years

stories.title: Tory Burch wants to help women add $1 billion to the economy by 2030
stories.summary: Tory Burch's foundation aims to add $1 billion to the economy through women entrepreneurs by 2030. Its fellows have already generated $470 million in revenue, showing the power of targeted support for women-led businesses.
stories.extended_summary: Women-led companies receive only 2% of venture capital funding, a gap the Tory Burch Foundation is working to close. Since launching its fellowship program in 2009, the foundation has supported over 500 women entrepreneurs, with 91% still in business after five years. In 2024, the foundation set an ambitious goal to add $1 billion to the economy by 2030—and less than a year later, its fellows have already reached nearly half that target. The program combines coaching, funding, and community to help women overcome systemic barriers.
stories.headline_split: Tory Burch wants to help women add
